words: LOLA GAMBLE CLYDE: Well, John Bond talked about it, about that there was afault that
ran from St. Maries on down along the Potlatch, along the Potlatch Canyon and that
it
runs on down to the Salmon River. And Pm not ageologist so 1 really don't know all
about it. But one day we stood on the rim of the Little Potlatch Canyon and he told
me
sort of, along there -along the Big Potlatch. And he says ere is afault there that
runs
from St. Maries to the Salmon River. Now that would be an interesting thing. I
don't think John Miller talked about that in his book, did he?
SAM SCHRAGER: No, I don't think so.
No. That would be an interesting thing to look up about, wouldn't it?
Yes. I've heard about those ice caves too. I've heard that there are some right
between Troy and Kendrick.
Yes, I've heard about that too. Well, it seems that there's this geological fault
that
runs along there. And Ithink that might be interesting because I'm not very good at
this earth sciences, you know, and I think that's something the kids would be very
interested in exploring,- about how this fault runs right along through their country.

speaker: SAM
words: I want to ask you about the play parties.

speaker: SAM
words: Oh, yes! The play parties. Well, it was customary on Friday evening in the rural

speaker: SAM
words: schools. With the coming of the pioneers, there was very little entertainment. There

speaker: SAM
words: were no shows, there were no cars, there was no television, there was no radio. So

speaker: SAM
words: they all had to make their own fun. So each person brought to the community their

speaker: SAM
words: own ethnic background. The people from different countries brought the singing games

speaker: SAM
words: of their land. And the people coming here, of course, to this country - they came

speaker: SAM
words: from

speaker: SAM
words: other states that had been settled. And each one brought in various forms of pioneer

speaker: SAM
words: games and that was one of the chief forms of amusement.

speaker: SAM
words: It was the Rathaus of those days. You went down to the schoolhouse - you didn't

speaker: SAM
words: choose their partners, you know. And this has been going on, of course, since the

speaker: SAM
words: days

speaker: SAM
words: of Adam and Eve, you know. So they had all kinds of games they'd play and most of

speaker: SAM
words: them were something about choosing partners. And if you weren't allowed to dance,

speaker: SAM
words: at

speaker: SAM
words: least they could swing them around alittle bit. And that served its purpose, you know.

speaker: SAM
words: ,of course, And some of the games they played werefHappy is the Miller Boy who lives

speaker: SAM
words: by the null,

speaker: SAM
words: and the wheel goes around of its own free will. One hand in the hopper and the other

speaker: SAM
words: in the sack. And we' 11 all turn together and we' 11 all turn back.  Everybody reverses

speaker: SAM
words: and the boy that's next to the girl grabs him a partner.

speaker: SAM
words: And another one went like this: "We're sailing East, we're sailing West, we're

speaker: SAM
words: sailing over the ocean and say young man, if you want to get awife - you've got to

speaker: SAM
words: be quick in the motion." And they grabbed the girl nearest them and away they went.

speaker: SAM
words: And of course, there was "Skip to My Lou My Darling" with all its various

speaker: SAM
words: verses. There must be forty-six verses to it all together. And everybody that came

speaker: SAM
words: from adifferent section of the country could add afew verses as it was sung in their

speaker: SAM
words: country.

speaker: SAM
words: How was "Skip to My Lou" played? Do you remember?

speaker: SAM
words: Yes. They all got in abig circle and you chose partners. And you stood and held

speaker: SAM
words: hands in this circle. And they sang about "My wife wears anumber ten shoe, my wife

speaker: SAM
words: wears anumber ten shoe, my wife wears anumber ten shoe, Skip to my Lou my

speaker: SAM
words: darling." And then you took ahold of your girlfriend and you swung her in acircle

speaker: SAM
words: round and round and round, you know. And then they'd get in abig circle again and

speaker: SAM
words: then when it came to "We' 11 all turn together and we' 11 all turn back," one stepped

speaker: SAM
words: ahead - the boy would step ahead and the girl would fall back. And that gave each

speaker: SAM
words: a new partner. Um-hum. And they played lots of other choosing partner

speaker: SAM
words: games. That was the best. And it was achance for the young people to meet

speaker: SAM
words: together and have fun under the very watchful eyes and chaperonage of the old folks.

speaker: SAM
words: Now they did this without music? They didn't have music?

speaker: LC
words: They sang. And they all mostly sang. Of course, in some of the very early, even

speaker: LC
words: the earliest people brought some forms of music, like violins. And some schoolhouses

speaker: LC
words: about the first thing they would buy would be a little reed organ. And with someone

speaker: LC
words: pumping on the organ you got alittle rhythm, alittle beat to it, you know. But if

speaker: LC
words: they didn't have anything they could still sing and clap, you know, clap in rhythm

speaker: LC
words: and

speaker: LC
words: that was all they needed. And when young people get together, you know, they

speaker: LC
words: can entertain themselves without much artifica. music and things. And they played

speaker: LC
words: guitars even in those days.

speaker: SAM
words: Did they? Oh,yes, lots of them had guitars and they would play guitars and sing.

speaker: SAM
words: Un-huh.

speaker: SAM
words: But I thought when they called it play parties, this is to mean that it isn't adance,

speaker: SAM
words: is that right?

speaker: SAM
words: Oh, well, no. It was these play party games were - there were other games too

speaker: SAM
words: that they played. They would play games like "Spin the Platter" and they'd get out

speaker: SAM
words: and^«

speaker: SAM
words: twirl apie pan around on the floor. And then they'd call out the name of some girl

speaker: SAM
words: and she'd run, see if she could catch it. And if she caught it why she maybe would

speaker: SAM
words: get aprize. Or if she didn't catch it then she had to be the twirler for the next

speaker: SAM
words: game.

speaker: SAM
words: And they played'charades"and everybody would act out something, you know. And you

speaker: SAM
words: acted out different games. And they would play - they'.;* play games like - and if

speaker: SAM
words: you

speaker: SAM
words: could guess it that would be fun. And if not you had to pay aforfeit. And some of

speaker: SAM
words: the

speaker: SAM
words: forfeits, you know, would be like kissing games. Some very pretty girl, she would

speaker: SAM
words: be

speaker: SAM
words: in great demand and she'd have to give aforfeit of kissing some of the boys there.

speaker: SAM
words: That caused great hilarity and great fun.

speaker: SAM
words: That was OK?

speaker: LC
words: That was OK. Yes, that was just fine. Even in those days. So you see they had

speaker: LC
words: a

speaker: LC
words: little - they had alittle something going on in those days (laughs)

speaker: LC
words: But I thought that in some places they played these play party games instead

speaker: LC
words: of dancing. They didn't approve of the regular kind of dances.

speaker: LC
words: Yes. Well, that was right. You could always get by playing "Skip to My Lou" and

speaker: LC
words: London Bridges Falling Down'" and "Happy is the Miller Boy" if they wouldn't allow

speaker: LC
words: you

speaker: LC
words: to dance. At least you could play these games and'you got to swing, your partners

speaker: LC
words: and girl in your arms tonight" was just about the same whether you were

speaker: LC
words: dancing "Black Bottoms" or if you were playing "Miller Boy."

speaker: SAM
words: What's "Black Bottoms?"

speaker: SAM
words: (laughs) Oh, that was some kind of adance they used to dance back many years

speaker: SAM
words: ago. Something like - what do they call 'em now? What are the dances the kids do

speaker: SAM
words: now? It isn't Watusi anymore is it? No - and its not Charleston. That was in my

speaker: SAM
words: age. I belonged to the flapper age and everybody was out doing the Charleston,

speaker: SAM
words: you know. But now do they do the Big Apple? No, that's out of style too.

speaker: SAM
words: No, they just stand there and move around.

speaker: LC
words: They just stand there and make motions - make suggestive motions - lets put it

speaker: LC
words: that way. They just stand there and go through suggestive motions.

speaker: SAM
words: Well, now, this was usually associated with the schoolhouse?

speaker: SAM
words: Yes, ah yes. Of course, they did meet in each others homes too. But the schoolhouse

speaker: SAM
words: had more room and you had more freedom. These schoolhouses generally had little

speaker: SAM
words: better lights than they had in their own private homes. Candlelight and one little

speaker: SAM
words: kerosene lamp weren't very conducive to big crowds. But the schoolhouse often

speaker: SAM
words: had agasoline lantern or something like that to light it up. And the young people

speaker: SAM
words: then are like the young people now - they didn't need too much light. They enjoyed

speaker: SAM
words: it

speaker: SAM
words: anyway. And who are we to say who had the most fun.you know.

speaker: SAM
words: 5AM: Do you think they had lots of fun in those days?

speaker: LC
words: Oh yes! I yes, I have abunch of letters from folks I taught fifty years ago

speaker: LC
words: every one of them was telling me how much they miss the good old times they

speaker: LC
words: had in the good old days. The good parties that they use to have. And that the

speaker: LC
words: schoolhouses were in use every day of the week. Saturday night they had their big

speaker: LC
words: play parties and on Sunday they had Sunday school.

speaker: LC
words: Did - would this mean say every Saturday night people would get together?

speaker: LC
words: Oh, just practically - yes - practically. They'd have surprise parties at each

speaker: LC
words: other's houses too. if the schools got too busy with their scheduled things then people

speaker: LC
words: would open their homes and the young folks and the old folks and the little folks

speaker: LC
words: would go to each others houses and have a party.

speaker: SAM
words: Would these sometimes last all night?

speaker: SAM
words: Oh I think so - yes. I can remember getting drifted in in one of my early school

speaker: SAM
words: teaching days. I remember the snow drifted so badly we couldn't^ Some

speaker: SAM
words: if we had wanted to so we stayed all night and went home in the morning. You know

speaker: SAM
words: the song about "and we danced all night to bread daylight and go home with the

speaker: SAM
words: girls in the morning." So they had little all night parties in those days.

speaker: SAM
words: Was it usually during the winter?

speaker: SAM
words: Usually in the winter because in the summer everybody was concerned with

speaker: SAM
words: putting in crops and harvesting the crops and that sort of thing. They didn't have

speaker: SAM
words: time

speaker: SAM
words: for much sociability.

speaker: SAM
words: So this made it a way of getting together, passing the time?

speaker: SAM
words: That's right. For the long winter evenings it broke the monotony. Right.

speaker: SAM
words: Now were there other thii«E that would be apart of the program besides the play

speaker: LC
words: Oh, yes - some - yes sometimes the schools would have what they called a"basket

speaker: LC
words: supper and all the girls made pretty baskets and brought them. And it was sort of

speaker: LC
words: acharity thing. If they needed some new records or if they needed avictrola or if

speaker: LC
words: they needed even just some new lamps. They would have abenefit and the girls

speaker: LC
words: would bring suppers and the boys would have to bid on them - on the baskets, to

speaker: LC
words: see who would get to eat with the girl of their choice. And the money would go

speaker: LC
words: to the school. And they had "pie suppers." Every girl would bake a pie and bring

speaker: LC
words: them. Much spirited bidding among the married men as well as the single men.

speaker: LC
words: And woe to the man feat didn't buy his own wife'spie! I'll tell you, that was bad,

speaker: LC
words: that was bad. And those were some of the things.

speaker: LC
words: Well, now lets say they were going to have aplay party night at school. Would

speaker: LC
words: they have things going on that night at the school before -?

speaker: LC
words: Yes, yes. Often the school children would start out with their program. They'd

speaker: LC
words: have like a little Thanksgiving program, maybe. And each would have a little

speaker: LC
words: recitation, maybe a little play about the coming of the Pilgrims - about why we

speaker: LC
words: should be thankful. And when that was over,then the other folks would shove

speaker: LC
words: back the chairs and maybe play singing games, as they called them. Or maybe

speaker: LC
words: play some play party games and they'd have lunch and then they'd go home. And

speaker: LC
words: that was the social life of the rural schools.

speaker: SAM
words: Were singing games the same as play party games?

speaker: SAM
words: Yes. The singing games were - well, they were the onesAyou sang to and

speaker: SAM
words: play party games could be anything like "Spin the Platter" and'Post Office" and

speaker: SAM
words: "Musical Chairs", or "Cross Questions and Silly Answers" and all those kind of

speaker: SAM
words: games where you didn't sing. The guessing games and that sort of thing or the

speaker: SAM
words: singing games.

speaker: SAM
words: That could be play party games

speaker: LC
words: Oh, yes, yes. In fact lots of people call play party games - they think of it

speaker: LC
words: only in terms of singing games. But that's not so, they were lots of other party

speaker: LC
words: games where you didn't necessarily sing. Like "Musical Chairs" and like "Spin

speaker: LC
words: the Platter," "Forfeits." Anything where you had to guess something, and get it wrong,

speaker: LC
words: then

speaker: LC
words: that was the paying of a forfeit for being wrong.

speaker: SAM
words: Where did these plays come from that these kids would put on?

speaker: SAM
words: Oh, well, sometimes they would make them up and those were the best of all,

speaker: SAM
words: if they had alittle history that they were interested in and could write alittle

speaker: SAM
words: pilgrim play. And some people could be the Indians and some pilgrims, and

speaker: SAM
words: others something else, you know. And that they wrote their own and what they thought

speaker: SAM
words: they

speaker: SAM
words: said. That would show alot of imagination and creativity. And they learned to spell,

speaker: SAM
words: incidentally, and use proper grammar and then they'd dramatize it. And put it on for

speaker: SAM
words: the

speaker: SAM
words: benefit of their folks. Sometimes they'd just learn pieces that they learned

speaker: SAM
words: regularly in school. Generally there were little verses that had agood moral

speaker: SAM
words: teaching to them, you know. And often they learned pieces like "Little Boy Blue"

speaker: SAM
words: and so on from their literature books. And then they would - whoever got to know

speaker: SAM
words: them the best and could speak them the best - they would get to elocute them for

speaker: SAM
words: their parents, for the enjoyment of their parents at the program - at the school program.

speaker: SAM
words: Did they ever put on "melo-dramers"?

speaker: SAM
words: Oh, yes, yes. Sometimes the young people of the community if they were ambitious

speaker: SAM
words: and felt the need of getting together, why they would have plays. And there was a

speaker: SAM
words: publishing company called the Sam French Company, and they turned out aregular

speaker: SAM
words: line of plays to be put on by rural schools. Didn't take much scenery. It didn't

speaker: SAM
words: take much costuming, and generally they were funny ones, you know, that would

speaker: SAM
words: appeal to the audience and send you home laughing.

speaker: SAM
words: What about the feed? Did they have abig feed when they had aplay party night?

speaker: LC
words: Oh yes, yes. And they often had - yes sometimes, generally sandwiches and

speaker: LC
words: cookies, and maybe cake, and coffee. Generally always coffee and that would be -

speaker: LC
words: sometimes the communities would get together, maybe for New Year's Day.

speaker: LC
words: Christmas Day they didn't very often get together but Thanksgiving and New Year's

speaker: LC
words: many of the communities made a regular habit of putting on abig Thanksgiving dinner

speaker: LC
words: at the schoolhouse and everybody come. And New Years was another favorite time

speaker: LC
words: for them all to get together and eat abig New Year's dinner and then dance in the

speaker: LC
words: evening - and the afternoon and evening - and visit. And those were good things to

speaker: LC
words: do with roads drifted and snow blowing. And people were very limited in how far

speaker: LC
words: they could go for a good time.

speaker: LC
words: Who was it that decided if there was going to be a night party? Would it be the

speaker: LC
words: teacher?

speaker: LC
words: Ithink generally it was the teacher that promoted it. That was part of their all-around

speaker: LC
words: bit of public relations - would be to see that the young folks had achance to get

speaker: LC
words: together and visit. And she was generally official chaperone. She and the school

speaker: LC
words: board would be the official chaperones for these parties and things. And that was

speaker: LC
words: kinda the way it worked out.

speaker: LC
words: What would they call the evening? An entertainment or aparty or what?

speaker: LC
words: Yes,yes. School entertainment. Sometimes they called it the school program. And

speaker: LC
words: sometimes it was just "we're going down to the school for alittle party." And

speaker: LC
words: sometimes there were women's clubs. Later on there got to be women's clubs in

speaker: LC
words: the communities and then the women's clubs were great at sponsoring some kind of

speaker: LC
words: adoings. And entertain their husbands, and the friends and the young people. But

speaker: LC
words: in the very oldest times, in the very earliest beginnings, I think it was largely

speaker: LC
words: sponsored by the teachers. Another thing they did almost before - before I was

speaker: LC
words: ateacher in the community - was having taffy pulls. And that was another chance

speaker: LC
words: for young people to get together, and socialize. And it didn't matter if theywere

speaker: LC
words: pulling candy or what they were doing. It was an opportunity to meet and^get

speaker: LC
words: acquainted. And so they served a real purpose, I think.

speaker: SAM
words: Did people of all ages dance in the play party games?

speaker: LC
words: Yes, they - well it was mostly the young people but it just depended on the

speaker: LC
words: community. In some of the communities the old people came too and they'd.et

speaker: LC
words: out - especially where they danced square dances, like "Virginia Reel" and all

speaker: LC
words: the old "swing 'em on the corner like you're swinging on the gate" - all the old

speaker: LC
words: dances where there were callers. There'd be a caller and then they'd generally

speaker: LC
words: had a violin and an organ, the school organ. And they'd have somebody who could

speaker: LC
words: play the fiddle. And they'd dance. And they did that from the beginning, I think,

speaker: LC
words: of

speaker: LC
words: homestead days, because someone wrote a very lovely poem about the boy playing the

speaker: LC
words: fiddle so "she could dance on the puncheon floor." And a puncheon floor, of course,

speaker: LC
words: is a sod floor where the sod's just been turned up. So that would kind a hard

speaker: LC
words: dancing but I guess they did it. Yah. Yah.

speaker: SAM
words: How long did they continue to have the school entertainments?

speaker: LC
words: Well, until the schools were consolidated. They did it up to the very last. And

speaker: LC
words: that's the one thing the rural people miss more than anything else - was it took away

speaker: LC
words: the center of their social life. The life of the community had centered around the

speaker: LC
words: schools - the dancing, the square dancing, the play party games, the entertainments

speaker: LC
words: marking the seasons, the interest in what their children were doing. So all those

speaker: LC
words: things entered into it. Now here comes my - now turn it off a minute, here comes

speaker: LC
words: my sister and Tom.
